intent: Check whether the candidate can choose between interface and type based on object contracts, unions, and declaration merging without false absolutes.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript? When would you use one over the other?

requirements.description: Both interface and type can describe ordinary object shapes.
Avoids claiming one is always correct for every model.
Interfaces can merge declarations in TypeScript.
This can be useful for augmentation but surprising if accidental.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions type aliases for unions and compositions.

requirements.description: Type aliases can represent unions, intersections, tuples, primitives, mapped and conditional types.
Unions such as Success | Failure require type alias.

expectedSignals.label: Uses project convention and API evolution as decision inputs.

requirements.description: Consistent local style matters.
Public extensible contracts may favor interface, while closed composed types may favor type.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Can an interface represent a union like Success | Failure directly?
Why can declaration merging be both useful and dangerous?

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands preserving type relationships across inputs and outputs.
primaryQuestion: What are generics in TypeScript? Write identity<T>(arg: T): T and explain why generics are useful over any.

requirements.description: Generic code works with multiple types while preserving type information.
T represents a type chosen by the caller or inferred by the compiler.
Input and output have the same type T.
The function preserves the relationship between argument and return value.
any loses type checking and allows unsafe operations.
Generics keep type safety and inference.
Arrays, API response wrappers, repositories, functions like first<T>, or reusable components.
Uses constraints when operations require a certain shape.

Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate can transform existing types safely instead of duplicating similar DTO shapes.
primaryQuestion: Explain Partial<T>, Required<T>, Pick<T, K>, Omit<T, K>, and Record<K, V> with use cases.

requirements.description: Partial makes all properties optional.
Required makes all properties required.
Pick selects a subset of keys.
Omit removes a subset of keys.
Record<K, V> maps keys K to values V.
Useful for dictionaries, lookup tables, or mapping statuses to config.

expectedSignals.label: Gives realistic DTO or config use cases.

requirements.description: Partial can model update DTOs carefully.
Omit can exclude passwordHash from public user type.
Pick can build lightweight response shapes.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can handle uncertain external data safely with narrowing instead of disabling type checking.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between unknown and any, and why is unknown safer?

expectedSignals.label: Explains any disables useful type checking.

requirements.description: any allows arbitrary property access and calls.
Errors can move from compile time to runtime.
unknown cannot be used as a specific type until checked.
Compiler forces typeof, instanceof, property checks, schema validation, or type guards.
API responses, JSON.parse, localStorage, message payloads, and catch errors are uncertain boundaries.
unknown is safer at these boundaries.
value as User can bypass safety without runtime proof.
Assertions should be close to validation or backed by invariant.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands decorators as metadata/annotation mechanisms used by NestJS for routing, DI, and validation.
primaryQuestion: What are TypeScript decorators, and why are they essential in NestJS?

expectedSignals.label: Defines decorators as declarations attached to classes, methods, parameters, or properties.

requirements.description: Decorators can add metadata or modify behavior depending on implementation.
They are evaluated by TypeScript/runtime support according to decorator model.
Class decorators such as @Controller or @Module.
Method decorators such as @Get or @Post.
Parameter decorators such as @Body or @Param.

expectedSignals.label: Connects decorators to metadata and framework discovery.

requirements.description: Nest scans metadata to build route maps and dependency graph.
Decorators make classes discoverable to the framework.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions decorators are not normal function calls in business code.

requirements.description: They describe structure and metadata declaratively.
Overusing custom decorators can hide behavior if not documented.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.

primaryQuestion: What is a type guard, and how do typeof, instanceof, and custom type guards with is work?

expectedSignals.label: Defines type guard as a runtime check that narrows a type.

requirements.description: Compiler uses control-flow analysis after checks.
Narrowing allows safe property or method access.
typeof narrows primitive-like runtime types such as string or number.
instanceof narrows values created by a constructor or class.
Function returning value is SomeType tells TypeScript about narrowed type when true.
The runtime check inside the guard must actually prove the shape.
A bad guard can lie to the compiler.
Runtime validation should match the claimed type.

intent: Check whether the candidate separates variable binding immutability from property assignment restrictions in type declarations.
primaryQuestion: What is the readonly modifier, and how does it differ from const?

expectedSignals.label: Explains const as variable binding restriction.

requirements.description: const prevents rebinding a variable.
It does not make object properties deeply immutable.

expectedSignals.label: Explains readonly as property assignment restriction.

requirements.description: readonly prevents assigning to a property through that type after initialization.
It is checked by TypeScript at compile time.
readonly can express immutable IDs, createdAt, or constructor-initialized fields.
DTOs can mark fields that should not be mutated after construction.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions shallow nature and runtime nuance.

requirements.description: readonly is not deep unless nested types are also readonly.
It does not automatically freeze runtime objects.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can balance readability, compiler help, and public API clarity.
primaryQuestion: What is type inference, and when should you rely on inference versus writing explicit types?

requirements.description: TypeScript can infer types from initializer, return expressions, and control flow.
Inference reduces redundant annotations.

expectedSignals.label: Names good places to rely on inference.

requirements.description: Local variables with obvious initializers.
Simple expressions where explicit type repeats the value.

expectedSignals.label: Names good places for explicit types.

requirements.description: Public function return types, exported APIs, complex objects, and boundary DTOs benefit from explicit types.
Explicit types can catch accidental return-shape changes.
Callback parameters may be inferred from surrounding API.
Explicit annotation can improve readability when inference is too complex.

Compiler can catch unhandled variants.

requirements.description: Enums emit JavaScript object-like runtime code.

expectedSignals.label: Explains const enum at a high level.

requirements.description: const enum is inlined by TypeScript in many configurations.

Union like "draft" | "active" has no runtime output by itself.
Pairs well with const arrays for runtime values.

expectedSignals.label: Chooses based on runtime needs and project convention.

requirements.description: Use union when no runtime enum object is needed.
Use enum when runtime namespacing or interop convention is valuable.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can derive types from object shapes and runtime constants safely.

requirements.description: keyof T produces a union of property names of T.
Useful for safe property access helpers or mapped types.
typeof value gets the TypeScript type of a value or constant.
It is different from JavaScript runtime typeof expression returning a string.
keyof typeof CONFIG derives keys from a runtime object.
Useful for config maps, labels, and finite value dictionaries.
Generic function get<T, K extends keyof T>(obj, key) can return T[K].
Prevents asking for keys that do not exist.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands safe compiler feedback and avoids silencing TypeScript at trust boundaries.

requirements.description: as SomeType tells the compiler to treat a value as a type.

Assertions at external boundaries can cause runtime crashes if data is wrong.
satisfies checks that a value conforms to a target type while preserving more specific inferred type.
Useful for config objects and finite maps.
strict enables safer checking such as strict null handling and stricter inference rules.
It catches bugs earlier in backend and frontend code.
